title: Brazil as the Empirical Center of SignWriting
subtitle: Documented institutional density, research opportunity, and why Brazil matters
summary: A compact evidence brief showing why Brazil is the strongest documented empirical center for SignWriting use, instruction, and future research.
synopsis: Brazil should not be treated as a side example in SignWriting conversations. It is the strongest documented empirical center currently visible: a distributed educational and university ecosystem, a deep research record, and a real base for future literacy, cognition, and infrastructure work. This deck states the 2018 documented floor clearly, explains what that floor does and does not prove, and shows why Brazil deserves to be treated as a major research center rather than as an anecdotal success story.

slides.title: Why Brazil Needs Its Own Brief
slides.summary: Brazil is no longer just one example among many. It is the strongest documented empirical center for SignWriting use and therefore deserves separate treatment.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for why Brazil deserves its own evidence brief.
bullets: Not an anecdote, but a documented center of activity
Educational, university, and research significance
A natural base for future literacy and infrastructure work
slides.speech: Brazil deserves to be treated separately because it is no longer merely a positive example. It is the strongest documented empirical center for SignWriting currently visible in the public record. That matters for two reasons at once. First, it shows that SignWriting already has real institutional density somewhere in the world. Second, it gives future research and infrastructure work a concrete place to begin rather than forcing everything to start from theory alone.
slides.notes: Open with scale and seriousness. This is an evidence brief, not a history lecture.
slides.mermaid:
graph TD
  A[Brazilian SignWriting Activity] --> B[Documented Institutional Density]
  B --> C[Research Opportunity]
  B --> D[Infrastructure Opportunity]
  C --> E[Empirical Center]
  D --> E

slides.title: The 2018 Accounting
slides.summary: A documented 2018 accounting identified 21 schools and universities in 13 Brazilian states with over 1,000 students tied to SignWriting use or training interest.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the 2018 Brazil accounting.
bullets: 21 schools and universities
13 Brazilian states
Over 1,000 students
Best treated as a documented floor, not a complete census
slides.speech: The strongest concise baseline comes from a 2018 project accounting connected to the Brazilian Sign Language Wikipedia effort. That document reported replies from 21 schools and universities across 13 Brazilian states with over 1,000 students. The safest and strongest way to use this number is as a documented floor. It is not a full national census, but it is already large enough to show that Brazil represents a distributed institutional center rather than a few isolated classrooms.
slides.notes: Be precise: documented floor, not exhaustive national count.
slides.mermaid:
graph LR
  A[2018 Documented Floor] --> B[21 Schools and Universities]
  A --> C[13 States]
  A --> D[1000+ Students]
  B --> E[Distributed Ecosystem]
  C --> E
  D --> E

slides.title: Not One Campus, But a Distributed Ecosystem
slides.summary: The documented network included both Deaf schools and universities, which makes Brazil more than a single local success story.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for Brazilian schools and universities using SignWriting.
bullets: Federal University of Santa Catarina research group
Federal University of Ceará partnership activity
Federal University of Maranhão training activity
Deaf-school use in Santa Maria and Ipiaú
slides.speech: The institutional shape matters. The 2018 documentation did not point to a single campus or one charismatic classroom. It named both universities and Deaf schools, including the Federal University of Santa Catarina, the Federal University of Ceará, the Federal University of Maranhão, and Deaf-school settings in places such as Santa Maria and Ipiaú. That mixture matters because it suggests a broader ecosystem of teaching, training, and research rather than a purely local experiment.
slides.notes: Emphasize distributed institutional presence, not exhaustive listing.

slides.title: Why This Matters Scientifically
slides.summary: Brazil matters because it offers real learner populations, real educational settings, and a real base for literacy, cognition, and dataset work.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the scientific significance of Brazilian SignWriting activity.
bullets: Real learner populations rather than hypothetical users
Repeated institutional settings rather than one-off trials
A base for literacy, cognition, corpus, and infrastructure studies
slides.speech: Brazil matters scientifically because it gives SignWriting research something unusually valuable: real learner populations in repeated institutional settings. That creates the possibility of better literacy studies, stronger classroom observation, more realistic dataset construction, and eventually more serious work in cognition, reading, and infrastructure. In other words, Brazil makes it easier to move from possibility claims toward actual empirical programs.
slides.notes: This is the research slide. Keep it concrete and build-oriented.

slides.title: Why This Matters Strategically
slides.summary: Brazil gives SignWriting a real evidence base that English-language academia has still not fully absorbed.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for the strategic significance of Brazil as a SignWriting center.
bullets: Evidence base already exists
English-language visibility remains too thin
Future grants can build from reality, not speculation
slides.speech: Brazil matters strategically because it changes the burden of proof. The question is no longer whether a signed writing system could ever gain serious institutional use somewhere. The question becomes why a documented Brazilian evidence base remains under-read in English-language science and higher education. That makes translation, synthesis, partnership, and infrastructure support much more valuable than purely abstract defense.
slides.notes: Keep the focus on under-read evidence, not on grievance.

slides.title: What Must Be Said Carefully
slides.summary: The Brazil case is strong, but it should still be described with disciplined boundaries.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for cautions and limits in the Brazil brief.
bullets: This is not a full national history
The 2018 count is not a complete census
Some broader educational claims still need program-level verification
The documented floor is already strong enough to matter
slides.speech: The strongest tone here is disciplined confidence. This brief does not attempt a full national history of SignWriting in Brazil, and it does not treat the 2018 accounting as a complete census. Some broader statements about current curricular requirements or nationwide practice would still need program-level verification. None of that weakens the central point. The documented floor is already large enough to justify treating Brazil as a major empirical center.
slides.notes: This slide protects the credibility of the whole brief.

slides.title: What Should Happen Next
slides.summary: The next task is not to admire the Brazil case, but to update it, connect it, and build research and infrastructure from it.
slides.image_alt: Placeholder image for next steps based on the Brazil evidence base.
bullets: Update the national accounting
Synthesize Brazilian work into English
Build partnerships, corpora, and study designs
Treat Brazil as a starting point for real infrastructure
slides.speech: The right response to the Brazil case is not passive admiration. It is follow-up. The accounting should be updated. The Brazilian literature should be synthesized more visibly in English. Partnerships should be built around actual institutions and learner populations. And the infrastructure work should be designed to support the research rather than lag behind it. That is how Brazil becomes not just evidence of past success, but a base for future science and public continuity.
slides.notes: End with action. This is where the deck shifts from documentation to program.
